content:
# Numbers Chapter 21 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 173-174 | Codex Vaticanus pa_0128-0129
**Source Images:** `NUM_021_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_021_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And the Canaanite king of Arad, who lived in the Negev, heard that Israel was coming by the way of Atharim. And he fought against Israel and took some of them captive.

**2** And Israel made a vow to YHWH and said, "If you will surely give this people into our hand, then we will utterly destroy their cities."

**3** And YHWH listened to the voice of Israel and gave over the Canaanites, and they utterly destroyed them and their cities. So the name of the place was called Hormah.

**4** And they set out from Mount Hor by the way of the Sea of Reeds, to go around the land of Edom. And the spirit of the people grew short on the journey.

**5** And the people spoke against God and against Moses: "Why have you brought us up out of Egypt to die in the wilderness? For there is no bread and there is no water, and our throats loathe this worthless food."

**6** And YHWH sent the fiery serpents among the people, and they bit the people, and many of the people of Israel died.

**7** And the people came to Moses and said, "We have sinned, for we spoke against YHWH and against you. Pray to YHWH, and let him take the serpents away from us." And Moses prayed on behalf of the people.

**8** And YHWH said to Moses, "Make for yourself a fiery serpent and set it on a pole. And it will be that everyone who has been bitten, when he looks at it, will live."

**9** And Moses made a serpent of bronze and set it on the pole. And it would happen that if a serpent had bitten anyone, and he looked at the bronze serpent, he would live.

**10** And the Sons of Israel set out and camped at Oboth.

**11** And they set out from Oboth and camped at Iye-abarim, in the wilderness that faces Moab toward the rising of the sun.

**12** From there they set out and camped in the Wadi Zered.

**13** From there they set out and camped on the other side of the Arnon, which is in the wilderness that extends from the border of the Amorites. For the Arnon is the border of Moab, between Moab and the Amorites.

**14** Therefore it is said in the Book of the Wars of YHWH: "Waheb in Suphah, and the wadis of the Arnon,

**15** and the slope of the wadis that extends toward the settlement of Ar, and leans against the border of Moab."

**16** And from there they went to Beer -- that is the well where YHWH said to Moses, "Gather the people together and I will give them water."

**17** Then Israel sang this song: "Spring up, O well! -- Sing to it! --

**18** The well that the princes dug, that the nobles of the people hollowed out, with the scepter, with their staffs." And from the wilderness they went to Mattanah,

**19** and from Mattanah to Nahaliel, and from Nahaliel to Bamoth,

**20** and from Bamoth to the valley that is in the country of Moab, at the top of Pisgah, which overlooks the wasteland.

**21** And Israel sent messengers to Sihon king of the Amorites, saying,

**22** "Let me pass through your land. We will not turn aside into field or vineyard. We will not drink water from any well. We will go by the King's Highway until we have passed through your territory."

**23** But Sihon would not allow Israel to pass through his territory. And Sihon gathered all his people and went out to meet Israel in the wilderness. And he came to Jahaz and fought against Israel.

**24** And Israel struck him with the edge of the sword and took possession of his land from the Arnon to the Jabbok, as far as the Sons of Ammon -- for the border of the Sons of Ammon was fortified.

**25** And Israel took all these cities, and Israel settled in all the cities of the Amorites, in Heshbon and in all its surrounding towns.

**26** For Heshbon was the city of Sihon king of the Amorites, who had fought against the former king of Moab and had taken all his land from his hand, as far as the Arnon.

**27** Therefore the ballad-singers say: "Come to Heshbon! Let it be built! Let the city of Sihon be established!

**28** For fire went out from Heshbon, a flame from the city of Sihon. It consumed Ar of Moab, the lords of the high places of the Arnon.

**29** Woe to you, Moab! You are destroyed, O people of Chemosh! He made his sons fugitives, and his daughters captives, to Sihon king of the Amorites.

**30** And we shot at them; Heshbon perished as far as Dibon. And we laid waste as far as Nophah, which reaches to Medeba."

**31** So Israel settled in the land of the Amorites.

**32** And Moses sent men to spy out Jazer, and they captured its surrounding towns and dispossessed the Amorites who were there.

**33** And they turned and went up by the way of Bashan. And Og king of Bashan went out to meet them -- he and all his people -- for battle at Edrei.

**34** And YHWH said to Moses, "Do not fear him, for I have given him into your hand -- him and all his people and his land. And you shall do to him as you did to Sihon king of the Amorites, who lived in Heshbon."

**35** And they struck him down, and his sons, and all his people, until there was no survivor left to him. And they took possession of his land.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v. 1-3 -- The Hormah vow and its fulfillment** (Heb. *va-yishma ha-Kena'ani melekh Arad yoshev ha-Negev ki va Yisrael derekh ha-Atarim*): The chapter opens with a military confrontation that bookends an earlier failure. In 14:45, the Amalekites and Canaanites at Hormah struck down Israel after a presumptuous attempt to enter the land; now, a generation later, the same region witnesses Israel's first military success in Canaan. The name *Hormah* derives from the root *ch-r-m* ("devote to destruction, place under the ban"), and the naming etiology in verse 3 -- *va-yiqra shem ha-maqom Hormah* -- is a wordplay: they "devoted to destruction" (*va-yacharemu*) the cities, so the place was called "Destruction" (*Hormah*). The key phrase in verse 2 is *im naton titten* -- the infinitive absolute construction ("if giving you will give," rendered here as "if you will surely give") that conveys emphatic certainty: Israel's vow is conditional, but the construction signals confidence in the outcome. The vow of *cherem* (total destruction) is among the most theologically difficult concepts in the Torah; it means that the conquered cities and their contents are "devoted" entirely to YHWH -- nothing is taken as plunder. The victory narrative is compressed into a single verse (v. 3), signaling that the narrator's interest lies not in military detail but in the theological pattern: vow-fulfillment-naming. The Leningrad Codex on page 173 (right column) presents these opening verses with clear parashah marking. The Vaticanus (pa_0128, continuing from the previous chapter in the third column) reads *kai ēkousen ho Chananeis basileus Arad* -- "and the Canaanite, king of Arad, heard." The LXX renders *cherem* with *anathematizō* ("devote to destruction, place under a curse"), preserving the sacral-destructive force of the Hebrew term. The term *ha-Atarim* is obscure -- it may be a place name, a common noun meaning "the routes" (from *tur*, "to explore"), or a reference to "the spies" (connecting to the reconnaissance route of chapter 13). The Leningrad text vocalizes it as a proper noun, which I follow.

**v. 4-5 -- The people's impatience and complaint** (Heb. *va-tiqtzer nefesh ha-am ba-darekh... va-yedabber ha-am be-Elohim u-ve-Mosheh*): The phrase *va-tiqtzer nefesh ha-am* is intensely physical: *nefesh* (throat, breath, life-force) became *qatzar* (short, cut short). The soul was "shortened" -- patience was exhausted, the inner capacity to endure was constricted to nothing. I render this "the spirit of the people grew short" to capture the visceral quality. The complaint follows the standard wilderness pattern (Exod 16:3, Num 11:4-6, 14:2-3) but with an escalation: the people speak *be-Elohim u-ve-Mosheh* -- "against God and against Moses," naming God directly rather than merely grumbling against Moses as intermediary. The phrase *ha-lechem ha-qeloqel* ("this worthless/miserable food") refers to the manna. The word *qeloqel* is rare, appearing only here in the Hebrew Bible, and seems to derive from *qalal* ("to be light, trifling, contemptible"). The people's sin is not merely complaining about discomfort but despising the provision of God -- calling divine sustenance "worthless." The Vaticanus renders *katelalēsen ho laos pros ton theon kai kata Mōusēn* -- "the people spoke against God and against Moses." The LXX uses *ton arton ton diakeron* ("this empty/vain bread") for *ha-lechem ha-qeloqel*, capturing the contemptuous tone.

**v. 6-9 -- The bronze serpent** (Heb. *va-yeshalach YHWH ba-am et ha-nechashim ha-seraphim... aseh lekha saraph ve-sim oto al nes*): This is one of the Torah's most theologically dense and paradoxical narratives. The *nechashim ha-seraphim* ("fiery serpents" or "burning serpents") combine two Hebrew words rich with resonance: *nachash* (serpent -- the same word as the creature of Genesis 3) and *saraph* (burning one -- the same root as the seraphim of Isaiah 6:2-6, the six-winged attendants of YHWH's throne). The serpents are thus creatures of both the ground and the divine court, agents of both death and the numinous. Their bite *burns* -- the venom produces a burning sensation, hence the name -- and the people die. The remedy is itself paradoxical: YHWH commands Moses to make a *saraph* (a fiery-serpent image) and place it on a *nes* (a pole or standard -- the same word used for a military banner or signal-flag). Anyone bitten who *looks* at the bronze serpent will live. The verb *hibbît* ("to look, gaze") implies not a casual glance but a deliberate, focused act of looking -- a turning of one's attention toward the divinely appointed remedy. The cure is not magical (the bronze image has no inherent healing power) but sacramental: the act of looking up at the pole is an act of obedient trust in YHWH's word. The rabbis (Rosh Hashanah 3:8) made this explicit: "Does a serpent kill or does a serpent heal? Rather, when Israel looked upward and subjected their hearts to their Father in heaven, they were healed; when they did not, they perished." The bronze serpent (*nechash ha-nechoshet*) involves an unmistakable wordplay: *nachash* (serpent) and *nechoshet* (bronze/copper) share the consonantal root *n-ch-sh*. Moses makes a serpent (*nachash*) out of bronze (*nechoshet*) -- the material echoes the creature. This artifact persisted in Israelite worship for centuries until Hezekiah destroyed it (2 Kings 18:4), where it is called *Nechushtan* -- a name that fuses "serpent" and "bronze" into a single word while also evoking *nachash* ("to practice divination"), suggesting it had become an object of illicit worship. The New Testament (John 3:14-15) reads this passage typologically: "As Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, so must the Son of Man be lifted up, that whoever believes in him may have eternal life" -- the bronze serpent on the pole becomes a type of the crucifixion, and *hibbît* ("looking") becomes *pisteuō* ("believing"). The Leningrad Codex on page 173 presents this unit with particular clarity, the *saraph* and *nachash* vocabulary clearly legible in the center column. The Vaticanus reads *poiēson seautō ophin kai thes auton epi sēmeiou* -- "make for yourself a serpent and set it upon a sign/standard." The LXX uses *sēmeion* ("sign") for *nes*, which adds a semiotic dimension absent from the Hebrew: the pole is not merely a support structure but a *sign* -- a visible marker that communicates meaning. The entire passage is a meditation on the paradox of homeopathic healing: the image of the thing that kills becomes the instrument of salvation.

**v. 10-13 -- The itinerary toward Moab** (Heb. *va-yis'u venei Yisrael va-yachanu be-Ovot... me-ever la-Arnon asher ba-midbar ha-yotze mi-gevul ha-Emori*): These verses constitute a travel itinerary -- one of the "station lists" that punctuate Numbers (cf. 33:1-49) -- tracing Israel's route around Edom and toward Moab. The stations are: Mount Hor (mentioned in 20:22) -> Oboth -> Iye-abarim ("ruins of the crossings," a name suggesting a place of passage or ancient ruins at a border crossing) -> Wadi Zered -> the far side of the Arnon. The Arnon (modern Wadi Mujib in Jordan) is a deep canyon that served as the natural boundary between Moab to the south and the Amorite territory to the north. The narrator provides a geographical gloss in verse 13: the Arnon *is* the border of Moab, *bein Mo'av u-vein ha-Emori* ("between Moab and the Amorites"). This gloss is critical for the subsequent narrative: Israel does not invade Moab (which YHWH has forbidden in Deut 2:9) but conquers Amorite territory north of the Arnon -- territory that the Amorites themselves had previously conquered from Moab (as the poem in vv. 27-30 will explain). The legal and moral justification for Israel's land-taking depends on this geographical precision. The phrase *Iye ha-Abarim* in verse 11 contains *Abarim* -- "the crossings" or "the regions beyond" -- the same name attached to the mountain range from which Moses will later view the promised land (27:12, Deut 32:49). The Vaticanus follows the same itinerary with minor orthographic variations.

**v. 14-15 -- The Book of the Wars of YHWH** (Heb. *al ken ye'amar be-sepher milchamot YHWH: et Vahev be-Suphah ve-et ha-nechalim Arnon*): This is one of the most tantalizing references in the Hebrew Bible -- a citation from a lost book. The *Sepher Milchamot YHWH* ("Book of the Wars of YHWH") is otherwise unknown; it presumably was a collection of war poems, victory songs, and geographical references celebrating YHWH's battles on Israel's behalf. Its existence demonstrates that the Torah's authors drew upon prior literary sources -- the Pentateuch is not a creation ex nihilo but a work of editorial compilation and theological composition. The quoted fragment is notoriously difficult. *Et Vahev be-Suphah* has no clear grammatical subject or verb; it may be a place name ("Waheb in Suphah"), a corrupt text, or a fragment whose beginning has been lost. The word *Suphah* may relate to *Yam Suph* ("Sea of Reeds"), suggesting a connection to the Exodus traditions. The "wadis of Arnon" (*nechalei Arnon*) and the "slope of the wadis" (*ve-eshed ha-nechalim*) that extends toward Ar (the Moabite settlement) describe the deeply incised canyon system of the Arnon. The word *eshed* (slope, waterfall, outpouring) is rare, occurring only here and in Deut 3:17. The fragment, broken and obscure as it is, serves the narrator's purpose: it establishes the Arnon as YHWH's war-boundary, the line beyond which Israel may legitimately advance. The Leningrad Codex on page 173 preserves this citation in the center and left columns. The Vaticanus presents a somewhat different reading, as is common with archaic poetic fragments where the Hebrew Vorlage may have already been difficult for the Greek translator.

**v. 16-18a -- The Song of the Well** (Heb. *az yashir Yisrael et ha-shirah ha-zot: ali ve'er enu lah*): The Well Song is one of the oldest poetic fragments in the Hebrew Bible, likely pre-monarchic. The formula *az yashir* ("then [he/they] sang") echoes the Song of the Sea in Exodus 15:1 (*az yashir Mosheh*), linking the two water-miracles: there, the sea that destroyed; here, the well that sustains. The song addresses the well directly: *ali ve'er* ("spring up, O well!") -- the imperative *ali* (from *'alah*, "to go up") personifies the well, commanding the water to rise. The phrase *enu lah* ("sing to it/answer it") invites a communal response -- this is antiphonal or call-and-response singing, the people addressing the earth itself, coaxing water from rock. The well was dug (*chafaruha*) by *sarim* ("princes, officials") and *nedivei ha-am* ("nobles of the people") with a *mechaqeq* ("scepter, commander's staff") and with their *mish'anotam* ("staffs, walking-sticks"). This is remarkable: the digging of the well is not attributed to Moses striking a rock (as in Exod 17:6 or Num 20:11) but to the communal leaders using their symbols of authority. The *mechaqeq* is both a ruling instrument and a digging tool -- a deliberate double meaning suggesting that leadership in Israel involves both governance and manual labor, both commanding and serving. The rabbinic tradition developed an extensive theology around this well, identifying it as the "Well of Miriam" (*be'erah shel Miryam*) that accompanied Israel through the wilderness and ceased flowing at Miriam's death (20:1-2) -- hence the water crisis of chapter 20 and the new provision here. The place name *Be'er* ("Well") in verse 16 is itself the common noun for "well," suggesting that the name commemorates the event. The Vaticanus reads *tote ēsen Israēl to asma touto epi tou phreatos: exarchete autō* -- "then Israel sang this song at the well: begin singing to it" -- using *exarchete* ("lead the singing, begin"), a term from Greek choral performance.

**v. 18b-20 -- The itinerary continued** (Heb. *u-mi-midbar Mattanah, u-mi-Mattanah Nachaliel, u-mi-Nachaliel Bamot, u-mi-Bamot ha-gay asher bi-sdeh Mo'av rosh ha-Pisgah ve-nishqaphah al penei ha-yeshimon*): The station names in this itinerary may function as both place names and common nouns, creating a theological wordplay: *Mattanah* means "gift," *Nachaliel* means "wadi/inheritance of God," and *Bamot* means "high places." Read as theology rather than geography, the journey traces a spiritual arc: from the wilderness (*midbar*), through gift (*mattanah*), to God's inheritance (*nachaliel*), to the heights (*bamot*), and finally to the summit of Pisgah overlooking the land. The rabbis (Eruvin 54a, Nedarim 55a) read the entire sequence allegorically as a journey of Torah study: from the wilderness (humility) through divine gift (Torah is freely given) to inheritance and elevation. The final station -- *rosh ha-Pisgah ve-nishqaphah al penei ha-yeshimon* ("the summit of Pisgah, which overlooks the wasteland") -- foreshadows Moses' final vista in Deuteronomy 34:1, where he will stand on Pisgah and see the promised land before his death. The *yeshimon* ("wasteland, desolation") is the barren landscape stretching below Pisgah toward the Dead Sea -- a word used elsewhere for the most extreme desolation (1 Sam 23:19, Ps 107:4). The Leningrad Codex on page 174 preserves this itinerary in the right column. The Vaticanus follows the same sequence of names with expected Greek transliterations.

**v. 21-24 -- The defeat of Sihon** (Heb. *va-yishlach Yisrael mal'akhim el Sichon melekh ha-Emori... va-yakkeihu Yisrael le-fi charev*): Israel's diplomatic approach to Sihon mirrors the earlier approach to Edom (20:14-21): a request for peaceful transit along the *derekh ha-melekh* ("King's Highway"), the major north-south trade route through Transjordan. The terms are identical: no trespassing into fields or vineyards, no drinking from wells, strict adherence to the road. But whereas Edom refused and Israel peacefully detoured (20:21), Sihon refuses and attacks -- and YHWH gives Israel the victory. The contrast establishes a moral framework: Israel sought peace; Sihon chose war; the consequences fall on Sihon. The phrase *va-yakkeihu Yisrael le-fi charev* ("Israel struck him with the edge of the sword" -- literally "to the mouth of the sword") uses the common but vivid biblical idiom of the sword as a devouring mouth. Israel takes Sihon's territory *min-Arnon ad Yabboq ad benei Ammon* -- from the Arnon (southern boundary) to the Jabbok (northern boundary, modern Wadi Zarqa) as far as the Ammonite border. The parenthetical note *ki az gevul benei Ammon* ("for the border of the Sons of Ammon was fortified") explains why Israel did not continue further: Ammon's border was strong (reading *az* as "strong, fortified" rather than the temporal "then"). Deuteronomy 2:19 provides the theological reason: YHWH forbade Israel from taking Ammonite territory. The Vaticanus reads *kai epataxen auton Israēl phonō machairēs* -- "and Israel struck him with the slaughter of the sword." The conquest of Sihon's kingdom is one of the most frequently remembered events in later Israelite literature (Deut 2:24-37, Josh 12:2, Judg 11:19-22, Ps 135:11, 136:19, Neh 9:22), serving as paradigmatic evidence of YHWH's faithfulness.

**v. 25-26 -- Heshbon as the Amorite capital** (Heb. *va-yiqach Yisrael et kol he-arim ha-elleh va-yeshev Yisrael be-khol arei ha-Emori be-Cheshbon u-ve-khol benoteiha*): The narrator specifies that Heshbon was Sihon's royal city (*ir Sichon melekh ha-Emori*) and provides the crucial historical backstory: Sihon had previously conquered this territory from *melekh Mo'av ha-rishon* ("the former king of Moab"). This detail is not merely historical but juridical. Israel did not take Moabite land; Israel took Amorite land that had once been Moabite but was no longer under Moabite sovereignty at the time of Israel's conquest. When Jephthah later disputes with the Ammonites over this territory (Judg 11:12-28), he cites exactly this argument: Israel took the land from the Amorites, not from Moab or Ammon. The phrase *benoteiha* ("its daughters") is the standard Hebrew idiom for subsidiary towns or villages dependent on a larger city -- the "daughter" towns of the "mother" city. The Leningrad Codex on page 174 presents these verses with the place names clearly legible.

**v. 27-30 -- The Heshbon Ballad** (Heb. *al ken yomru ha-moshlim: bo'u Cheshbon tibbane ve-tikonen ir Sichon*): The *moshlim* are "ballad-singers" or "proverb-speakers" (from *mashal*, "proverb, parable, taunt-song"). The poem they recite is an ancient Amorite victory song celebrating Sihon's original conquest of Moab -- which the Israelite narrator quotes with devastating irony. The poem praises Sihon's conquest of Moab, but the reader knows that Sihon himself has now been conquered by Israel. The taunt-singer's boast has become Sihon's epitaph. Verse 27: *bo'u Cheshbon* ("come to Heshbon!") -- originally an Amorite invitation to rebuild the conquered city as Sihon's capital. *Tibbane ve-tikonen* ("let it be built, let it be established") uses passive forms suggesting a communal rebuilding effort. Verse 28: *ki esh yatze'ah me-Cheshbon, lehavah mi-qiryat Sichon* ("for fire went out from Heshbon, a flame from the city of Sihon") -- the fire is Sihon's military power, which consumed *Ar Mo'av* (Ar of Moab) and the *ba'alei bamot Arnon* ("lords/masters of the high places of the Arnon"). The *bamot* here are likely both topographical (the heights along the Arnon canyon) and cultic (the worship sites on those heights). Verse 29: *oy lekha Mo'av* ("woe to you, Moab!") -- the woe-oracle form. Moab is called *am Kemosh* ("people of Chemosh"), identifying Moab with its national deity. Chemosh is well attested in extra-biblical sources, most notably the Mesha Stele (ninth century BCE), where King Mesha of Moab attributes his victories to Chemosh using language almost identical to Israelite attributions to YHWH. The claim that Chemosh *gave* (*natan*) his sons as fugitives (*peletim*) and his daughters as captives (*shivyot*) to Sihon attributes Moab's defeat to Chemosh's abandonment of his own people -- a theological explanation that mirrors the Mesha Stele's logic in reverse. Verse 30: This verse is textually the most difficult in the chapter. The MT reads *va-niram... Cheshbon ad Divon va-nashim ad Nophach asher ad Medeva* -- the verb *va-niram* is obscure, possibly from *yarah* ("to shoot") or a textual corruption. I render "we shot at them" following the reading *va-niram* as a first-person plural from *yarah* (with a military sense), though other interpretations are possible ("we overthrew them," "their fire was quenched"). *Nophah* (*Nophach* in some readings) is otherwise unattested; the LXX reads differently. The entire verse may be a continuation of the Amorite taunt-song or the narrator's editorial summary. The Vaticanus presents notable divergences in this poem, as is typical of archaic Hebrew poetry where the Greek translator faced a Vorlage already obscure.

**v. 31-32 -- Settlement and reconnaissance** (Heb. *va-yeshev Yisrael be-eretz ha-Emori... va-yishlach Mosheh le-ragel et Ya'zer*): The summary statement *va-yeshev Yisrael be-eretz ha-Emori* ("so Israel settled in the land of the Amorites") marks the completion of the Sihon conquest. Moses then sends scouts (*le-ragel*, "to spy out" -- from *regel*, "foot," hence "to go on foot, to reconnoiter") to Jazer (a city north of Heshbon). The verb *ragal* is distinct from *tur* (used in chapter 13 for the failed spy mission); the different vocabulary may signal a different kind of reconnaissance -- tactical rather than strategic. They capture Jazer's dependent towns (*benoteiha*, "its daughters") and dispossess (*va-yoresh*) the Amorites -- the hiphil of *yarash* meaning to cause to inherit in reverse, to drive out the possessors. The Jazer operation is a limited military action, not a major campaign, but it secures Israel's northern flank before the advance toward Bashan.

**v. 33-35 -- The defeat of Og** (Heb. *va-yifnu va-ya'alu derekh ha-Bashan va-yetze Og melekh ha-Bashan liqratam... va-yakku oto ve-et banav ve-et kol ammo*): The Og narrative is compressed to three verses but carries enormous weight in Israel's memory. Og king of Bashan is one of the most legendary figures in the Torah, later described as the last of the Rephaim (Deut 3:11), a race of giants, whose iron bed measured nine by four cubits. The name *Bashan* (modern Golan Heights and Hauran plateau in Syria) evokes rich agricultural land -- oak forests, cattle pastures, and volcanic basalt plains. Og comes out *liqratam* ("to meet them") at Edrei (modern Dera'a in southern Syria), and YHWH addresses Moses with the emphatic reassurance: *al tira oto* ("do not fear him"). The command not to fear implies that there was reason to fear -- Og was formidable. The divine promise uses the prophetic perfect: *ki ve-yadkha natatti oto* ("for I have given him into your hand") -- the past tense for a future event, expressing certainty. The instruction *ve-asita lo ka'asher asita le-Sichon* ("do to him as you did to Sihon") establishes a pattern: the Sihon victory is the template for the Og victory. The execution is reported with devastating brevity: *va-yakku oto ve-et banav ve-et kol ammo ad bilti hish'ir lo sarid* ("they struck him and his sons and all his people until no survivor remained to him"). The phrase *ad bilti hish'ir lo sarid* is formulaic for total military defeat (cf. Deut 3:3, Josh 10:33). The chapter ends with *va-yirshu et artzo* ("and they took possession of his land"), the verb of dispossession-and-inheritance (*yarash*) that carries the entire Deuteronomistic theology of land: YHWH gives, Israel receives, the prior inhabitants are displaced. The Leningrad Codex on page 174 presents these final verses in the left column. The Vaticanus reads *mē phobēthēs auton hoti eis tas cheiras sou paradedōka auton* -- "do not fear him, for into your hands I have delivered him." The twin victories over Sihon and Og become a fixed pair in Israel's liturgical memory, celebrated in Psalms 135:11 and 136:19-20 (*le-Sichon melekh ha-Emori... u-le-Og melekh ha-Bashan*) with the refrain *ki le-olam chasdo* ("for his steadfast love endures forever"). The chapter that began with defeat and captivity (v. 1) ends with conquest and possession (v. 35) -- a narrative arc from vulnerability to dominion, mediated throughout by YHWH's intervention: in serpents and healing, in wells and songs, in battles and victories.

**Structural note on chapter 21 in its context**: Numbers 21 is the great pivot of the book. Everything before it -- the complaints, the serpents, the wandering -- belongs to the wilderness generation and its failures. Everything after it -- the conquests, the Balaam cycle, the settlement of Transjordan -- belongs to the new generation and its successes. The chapter itself contains the transition: the bronze serpent episode (vv. 4-9) is the last of the wilderness complaint-and-punishment narratives, while the Sihon and Og conquests (vv. 21-35) are the first of the conquest narratives. Between them lies the itinerary (vv. 10-20) with its embedded poetry -- the Book of the Wars and the Well Song -- marking the geographical and thematic passage from wilderness to promised land. The chapter's three poetic fragments (the Well Song, the Wars citation, the Heshbon Ballad) form a literary triptych: ancient sources woven into the prose narrative to authenticate the geographical and military claims. The bronze serpent, positioned at the chapter's opening, provides the theological key: the people who look up in trust are healed; the nation that advances in obedience is victorious. The serpent on the pole and the victories in battle are two expressions of the same reality -- YHWH's power to save those who turn their gaze toward him.
